title: Record the First Community Site Snapshot
summary: Carry the staged community-site proposal into its first commit, then inspect the new history entry and confirm the working tree is clean.

sketchBlocks.title: Initialize community-site and set a repo-local identity
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
Worked example: Start from an ordinary folder and give Git a local identity before you make any commits. A local identity keeps this repository's commits attributable without changing your global Git settings.

Example scenario (different repo name so you won't accidentally repeat it in Try It):

    mkdir meetup-site
    cd meetup-site
    git init -b main
    git config --local user.name "Alice Example"
    git config --local user.email "alice@example.org"

Why this matters: git init creates the .git database and the initial branch (main). Setting user.name and user.email with --local stores the identity in this repository only, which is a safe beginner practice when you share a machine. After these commands, git status shows the working tree and any untracked files; there is no commit yet, so history is empty.

Interpreting the result: After git init -b main the repository exists and HEAD points at an empty main. The local config entries are visible with git config --local --list. A common beginner mistake is to forget --local and accidentally change global identity; using --local prevents that.

sketchBlocks.title: Move the proposal files into the staging area
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
Worked example: Staging collects specific files you want into a single focused snapshot. Use git add to stage files and git status --short to inspect exactly what's staged.

Example scenario (uses different filenames than the real community-site Try It):

    # in a different example folder
    git add draft.txt plans/overview.md
    git status --short

Why this matters: Staging lets you prepare only the relevant changes for a commit. The staged snapshot is what git commit records. If you forget to stage, the commit will be empty or only include files that were staged. The status output shows an "A" for added files in the index and "??" for untracked files in the working tree.

Interpretation: A line that starts with "A " means the file is staged for addition. " M" (space then M) would mean the file is modified in the working tree but not staged.

sketchBlocks.title: Commit the staged proposal with the required message
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
Worked example: Make a focused commit message that explains the snapshot. The example below intentionally uses a different commit message than the Try It so learners practice the same steps without reproducing this exact example.

Example scenario (different commit message):

    git commit -m "Initial import of website drafts"

Why this matters: The commit message becomes the concise human-readable history entry. For this topic, learners will be asked to use a specific message so that automated checks can verify the intended record. A common error is typing a message with a typo or extra punctuation; automated checks expect the exact message text used in the lesson.

Interpretation: After the commit, git log --oneline shows a one-line summary. The working tree should become clean if everything staged was committed.

sketchBlocks.title: Inspect the first history entry and verify no pending changes
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
Worked example: Use git log --oneline to read the concise history and git status --short (or git status) to check there are no staged or unstaged changes.

Example commands (different repository example):

    git log --oneline
    git status --short

Why this matters: Reading history is a non-destructive operation — it shouldn't change repository state. Verifying the working tree is clean ensures your snapshot recorded exactly what's intended and you don't accidentally carry unrelated work forward. A common trap is running a command that modifies the index; for reading history you only need read-only commands.

Interpretation: git log --oneline lists commits with the newest at top. git status --short returning no lines means the working tree and index are both clean.

quizDraft.title: Why record local history instead of duplicate final folders?
quizDraft.prompt: Which single answer best explains why a local Git history is better than copying the current folder to a new folder named with a date?
options: A dated folder is simpler and always preferable for backups.
Git records small, focused snapshots with messages and authors, making history inspectable without duplicating entire folders.
Copying folders creates a readable log that Git cannot match.
A dated folder automatically shows who changed each file and why.
quizDraft.hint: Think about tracking changes over time and the ability to see specific snapshots with context.

help.concept: Version control records small, named snapshots and who made them; it avoids duplicate directory clutter and preserves a compact history you can inspect precisely.
help.hint_1: A dated copy creates many folders and doesn't record the sequence of incremental changes or a concise author/message for each snapshot.
help.hint_2: Pick the option that highlights concise change recording, authorship, and inspectable history rather than creating extra folders.

quizDraft.title: Where should you set the repository identity for this lesson?
quizDraft.prompt: When preparing the first commit in the course repository, which Git config command reflects the recommended practice taught here?
options: git config --local user.name "ZoeSkoul Learner" && git config --local user.email "learner@zoeskoul.local"
git config user.email "learner@zoeskoul.local" without specifying --local or --global
Edit ~/.gitconfig by hand to add the repository name
quizDraft.hint: We want the identity to apply only to this repository, not change the user's global settings.

help.concept: git config --local writes identity into the repository-local config so the setting applies only to that repository.
help.hint_1: Look for the option that uses --local and sets both name and email.
help.hint_2: Avoid options that change the global identity.

quizDraft.title: Which status lines indicate files are staged?
quizDraft.prompt: Select all lines from git status --short that indicate a file is staged (already in the index) rather than only changed in the working tree.
quizDraft.hint: Use the lesson explanation and the wording of this question to narrow the answer.

help.concept: This support text was repaired because the original wording revealed the answer too directly.
help.hint_1: Compare the question to the lesson example and remove details that do not match the requested concept.
help.hint_2: Use the role or evidence named in the prompt instead of relying on answer wording.

quizDraft.title: Order the steps to create the first commit
quizDraft.prompt: Arrange the steps in the correct order to produce the first commit of a repository from an ordinary folder.
tokens: Create the repository with git init -b main
Set repository-local user.name and user.email with git config --local
Stage selected files with git add <files>
Record the staged snapshot with git commit -m "…"
correctOrder: Create the repository with git init -b main
Set repository-local user.name and user.email with git config --local
Stage selected files with git add <files>
Record the staged snapshot with git commit -m "…"
quizDraft.hint: Think of repository creation, local identity, staging, then recording the snapshot.

help.concept: The correct sequence is: initialize the repo (so .git exists), configure any repository-local identity, stage the chosen files into the index, then run git commit to record the snapshot.
help.hint_1: git init comes before git config --local when you want the config stored in the repo.
help.hint_2: You must stage (git add) before git commit to include files in the recorded snapshot.

quizDraft.title: Choose the exact first commit message
quizDraft.prompt:
The course checks expect the first commit message to be: [blank1]

Pick the exact message used by the automated checks.
quizDraft.hint: Match punctuation and lower/upper case exactly.

help.concept: Automated checks compare the commit message text exactly, so the message must match the expected string character for character.
help.hint_1: Look for the full string used in the Try It commit command (it includes a colon and hyphens as typed).
help.hint_2: Do not add extra spaces or different capitalization; select the exact option.

quizDraft.title: Fill the local email configuration command
quizDraft.prompt: Set the repository-local email using: git config --local user.email [blank1]
quizDraft.hint: Pick the email the course expects for the repository-local identity.

help.concept: git config --local user.email sets the email used in commits created in that repository (stored in .git/config). The expected email here is the deterministic learner identity provided in the course.
help.hint_1: The course uses a deterministic learner email so checks are reproducible.
help.hint_2: Choose the exact email 'the missing term'.

quizDraft.title: Initialize the community-site repo and set the repo-local identity
quizDraft.prompt:
You have an ordinary folder named community-site with proposal files inside. Use the terminal to initialize a Git repository on branch main and set a repository-local name and email.

Type the commands and press Enter. After your commands, run git status --short so we can see the repository state.

The terminal opens inside `community-site/` with the required starting milestone ready. Type each requested terminal command and press Enter.
quizDraft.hint: Read the Git task "Initialize the community-site repo and set the repo-local identity" and identify the repository state you need to create.

help.concept: This Git exercise checks the repository state produced by "Initialize the community-site repo and set the repo-local identity".
help.hint_1: Use git status before and after each small change so you can see what moved between the working tree, staging area, and history.
help.hint_2: Match the requested file names, branch names, and commit purpose before clicking Check Answer.

quizDraft.starterCode:
# Work in the terminal; the starting repository state is ready.
# Type each requested command and press Enter.
quizDraft.instructions:
You have an ordinary folder named community-site with proposal files inside. Use the terminal to initialize a Git repository on branch main and set a repository-local name and email.

Type the commands and press Enter. After your commands, run git status --short so we can see the repository state.

The terminal opens inside `community-site/` with the required starting milestone ready. Type each requested terminal command and press Enter.

starterFiles.content:
# Work in the terminal; the starting repository state is ready.
# Type each requested command and press Enter.

#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -eu
rm -rf -- "community-site"
mkdir -p -- "community-site"
printf '%s\n' 'Cumulative Git practice project' > "community-site/README.md"
git -C "community-site" init -q -b main
git -C "community-site" config user.name "ZoeSkoul Learner"
git -C "community-site" config user.email "learner@zoeskoul.local"
git -C "community-site" add -- "README.md"
git -C "community-site" commit -q -m "Start cumulative project"

solutionFiles.content:

git -C "community-site" config user.name "ZoeSkoul Learner"
git -C "community-site" config user.email "learner@zoeskoul.local"
git -C "community-site" add -- "README.md"
git -C "community-site" commit -q -m "Start cumulative project"

quizDraft.title: Stage all five proposal files into the index
quizDraft.prompt:
The community-site repository is initialized and has a repo-local identity, but none of the proposal files are staged. Stage these five files into a single snapshot: README.md, proposal.md, index.html, LICENSE, and NOTES.md. After staging, run git status --short and press Enter.

The terminal opens inside `community-site/` with the required starting milestone ready. Type each requested terminal command and press Enter.
quizDraft.hint: Read the Git task "Stage all five proposal files into the index" and identify the repository state you need to create.

help.hint_1: Use git status before and after each small change so you can see what moved between the working tree, staging area, and history.

quizDraft.prompt:
Inspect the repository history and confirm there are no staged or unstaged changes. From the community-site folder run the command that shows the concise one-line log and then run the command that shows short status output. Type each command and press Enter.

You should NOT change any files for this check.

The terminal opens inside `community-site/` with the required starting milestone ready. Type each requested terminal command and press Enter.

#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -eu
rm -rf -- "community-site"
mkdir -p -- "community-site"
printf '%s\n' 'Cumulative Git practice project' > "community-site/README.md"
git -C "community-site" init -q -b main
git -C "community-site" config user.name "ZoeSkoul Learner"
git -C "community-site" config user.email "learner@zoeskoul.local"
git -C "community-site" add -- "README.md"
git -C "community-site" commit -q -m "Start cumulative project"
printf '%s\n' 'Prepared practice change' >> "community-site/README.md"
git -C "community-site" add -- "README.md"
